content: Diagnosis: fasting serum urate measurement (non-fasting acceptable but postprandial levels may be slightly lower). Confirm with repeat testing if asymptomatic, as levels fluctuate. Normal ranges vary by laboratory and sex: men 3.4-7.0 mg/dL, women 2.4-6.0 mg/dL (premenopausal). Evaluate for secondary causes: medication review, assess alcohol intake, dietary history, renal function (BUN, creatinine, eGFR), complete blood count (myeloproliferative disorders), metabolic panel. Consider 24-hour urine uric acid excretion or fractional excretion of uric acid to distinguish overproduction vs underexcretion if etiology unclear or considering uricosuric therapy. Imaging for kidney stones if symptomatic (renal ultrasound, CT). Genetic testing not routinely indicated but research identifies polymorphisms in urate transporters. Assess for gout symptoms even if not reported (joint pain, tophi). Screen for associated conditions: blood pressure, lipids, glucose/HbA1c given metabolic syndrome association.
